title: Build Powerful AI Without Coding

modules.title: What Is a Workflow and Why Build One?
modules.sub: Mapping how tasks connect to solve real problems
modules.description: Learners step into the role of a startup founder and explore what a workflow actually is by breaking down everyday processes into inputs, actions, and outputs. They map a simple business problem visually and decide which parts could benefit from automation. By the end, they have a clear problem statement to carry through the rest of the course.

modules.title: Connecting Tools: Inputs and Outputs
modules.sub: Linking no-code tools so data flows automatically
modules.description: Learners experiment with at least two no-code platforms and trace how data moves from one tool to another through triggers and actions. They build a small test connection — for example, a form submission that sends an automatic message — and observe what happens when the chain works and when it doesn't. This hands-on tinkering builds intuition for how automated systems are wired together.

modules.title: Design a Real Automation Challenge
modules.sub: Turning your startup problem into a buildable workflow plan
modules.description: Learners take their problem statement and design a multi-step workflow blueprint, deciding which tasks a human should own and which an AI-powered tool can handle. They make deliberate choices about tool selection, sequence, and conditions, documenting their reasoning like a real product designer. The module ends with a workflow diagram ready to be built and tested.

modules.title: When the Workflow Breaks â Debug It
modules.sub: Finding and fixing the weak links in your automation
modules.description: Learners are presented with a deliberately broken or misfiring workflow and must diagnose what went wrong by reading error messages and tracing the data path step by step. They apply a simple debugging framework — isolate, test, fix, retest — to get their automation running correctly again. This module builds resilience and the practical mindset that failure is a normal part of building.

modules.title: Ethics Check: Should This Be Automated?
modules.sub: Deciding when handing decisions to AI goes too far
modules.description: Learners audit their own workflow through an ethics lens, asking who could be harmed, what bias might creep in, and whether any step removes important human judgment. They identify at least one genuine ethical risk their workflow creates and propose a concrete safeguard or design change to address it. The activity shifts them from pure builders into critical thinkers responsible for what they make.

modules.title: Launch Your Workflow, Present Your Case
modules.sub: Pitching your live automation to a tough boardroom panel
modules.description: Learners run their completed workflow live and prepare a short boardroom-style pitch explaining the problem it solves, the tools used, and the ethical risk they identified and managed. They defend their design decisions against challenging questions, just as a real founder would in front of investors. The module serves as the course capstone, integrating technical skill, design thinking, and ethical reasoning into one confident presentation.

synopsis: You're a startup founder with a real problem and zero engineers â so you must build an AI-powered workflow using no-code tools to solve it before your pitch meeting. Each module adds a new layer of complexity, and you'll defend every automation decision in a final board-room scenario.
rationale: No-code AI tools are rapidly democratizing automation for careers and entrepreneurship, but teenagers have almost no structured exposure to workflow thinking, tool chaining, or the ethical questions automation raises.
learning_outcome: Students can design, build, and critically evaluate a multi-step AI workflow using at least two no-code tools, and articulate one ethical risk their workflow creates.
source_signals: Launch HN: AgentHub (YC W24) â A no-code automation platform
